steps.text: Clear a square of floor in {room} big enough for you and {friend}.
Gather four pillows and two blankets and stack them in your spot.
Stand the pillows on edge to make four fort walls.
Drape one blanket across the top for the roof.
What kind of fort is this for {friend}?
Tuck a book and a flashlight inside. Crawl in with {friend} and read one page out loud.
Cut a peephole between two pillows. Take turns spying out with {friend} and call out three things you see.
Gather two sheets of paper and one crayon. Sit at a flat surface with {friend}.
Fold the first sheet in half the long way, then unfold it.
Fold both top corners down to meet the center crease.
Fold the slanted edges down again to make a sharp nose.
What kind of plane is this?
Fold the wings flat and crisp the creases with your thumbnail.
Bend the back wing tips up a little so the plane wants to climb.
Color a number on each wing — that is your plane's flight number.
Hold the plane behind your ear, point at {friend}, and let it fly. Cheer when it lands!
{friend} wants to play a color hunt. Pick a color to chase first.
Walk slowly around {room} looking for red. Touch each red thing and count out loud.
Walk slowly around {room} looking for blue. Touch each blue thing and count out loud.
Walk slowly around {room} looking for yellow. Touch each yellow thing and count out loud.
Bring {count} of your favorites back and lay them in a line in front of {friend}.
Tell {friend} which one was the easiest to find and which was the trickiest.
Find a tray, plate, or shoebox lid to be your garden frame.
Collect six small pebbles, beads, or buttons and pour them in.
Add one leaf, one twig, or one folded paper triangle as a tree.
Push the pebbles around to make a path winding through the middle.
What lives in this garden with {friend}?
Pile three pebbles into a dragon hill at the path's end. Place {friend} as the dragon's friend.
Make four small pebble nests off the path. Tuck {friend} in as the kindly grandmouse.
Gather {count} small objects from {room} into a messy pile near {friend}.
How should we sort the pile?
Line everything up smallest on the left, biggest on the right.
Line everything up palest on the left, darkest on the right.
Line everything up softest on the left, hardest on the right.
Walk {friend} along the line and announce each thing in order.
Lay paper and three crayons in front of you. {friend} is the art director.
Draw a big round body in the middle of the page.
Add {count} eyes anywhere on the body.
Give the monster four legs — straight, wiggly, or zig-zag, your call.
What does your monster eat?
Draw three purple snacks floating near the monster's mouth.
Draw three perfectly round snacks floating near the monster's mouth.
Whisper the monster's name to {friend}, then say it loud and proud.
Stand in {room} with {friend} and look at the open floor.
What kind of course should we build?
Set two pillows as crawl-under tunnels and one rolled blanket as a jump bar.
Lay a long string or belt on the floor as a wobbly tightrope and place a book at each end.
Walk the course slowly first to remember every station.
Run the course as fast as you can while {friend} cheers from the sidelines.
Take a bow. Tell {friend} the hardest part and the easiest part.
Find a cup, a bowl, or a jar to be your cauldron.
Add three pretend ingredients from {room} — a sock, a paper scrap, a crayon stub.
Stir slowly with a spoon or stick while you whisper the recipe to {guide_mentor}.
What does this potion do?
Pretend to drink it, then run a victory lap around {room}.
Pretend to drink it, then crouch behind a {prop} and giggle while {guide_mentor} 'looks' for you.
Pretend to pour it on a houseplant, then slowly stand tall with arms out like branches.
Sit on the floor with paper, a crayon, and {friend} the cartographer.
Draw the outline of {room} as a big rectangle on the page.
Add the door, the window, and one piece of furniture as boxes inside.
Pick a hiding spot in {room} and hide a small {prop} there.
Draw a big X on the map at the hiding spot.
How does the treasure hunter find the X?
Draw {count} numbered footprints from the door to the X. Hand the map to a grown-up to follow.
Write one clue word at the top of the map. Hand the map to a grown-up and watch them puzzle it out.
Set up a pretend stovetop on a low table or chair seat with {friend} as the customer.
Gather four pretend ingredients — pebbles, paper balls, leaves, anything.
What is on the menu today?
Drop the ingredients into a pretend pot. Stir slowly and hum a cooking tune.
Press the ingredients flat onto a pretend pan. Flip each one with a big wrist toss.
Slide your dish onto a small plate or paper square in front of {friend}.
Take {friend}'s order review — five stars or four? Bow to the chef!
Find a tall cardboard box, paper-towel tube, or stacked cushions to be your rocket body.
Tape or balance a paper triangle on top as the nose cone.
Add three paper rectangles around the base as fins.
Strap {sidekick} into the rocket as the brave astronaut.
Count down from ten with one finger raised per number.
Where does the rocket fly today?
Bounce around {room} on tip-toe like low gravity. Plant a paper flag wherever you land.
Crouch low, then stretch up tall like blooming. Tell {sidekick} which giant flower is friendliest.
Collect paper-towel tubes, books, and a small ball or marble.
Lean two books to make a ramp from a chair down to the floor.
Lay one tube along the ramp so the marble can roll through it.
Where should the marble land at the bottom?
Tape a paper cup at the end of the tube. Aim carefully and roll the marble in.
Place a pillow under the tube's end. Watch the marble bounce-soft and stop.
Drop the marble three times in a row and count how many times it lands cleanly.
Tell {friend} the number out loud and high-five for the best run.
Find a small flat surface — a tray, a windowsill, a low shelf — for the display.
Walk through {room} (or near a window) and gather {count} natural-feeling things: leaves, pebbles, pinecones, feathers.
Lay everything out on the surface in any order.
Pick your favorite find and place it in the very center.
How should we share the display with {friend}?

choices.label: Make a tiny label for each

steps.text: Tear small paper squares and write or draw a label per item. Place each label beside its find.
Pick up each item one at a time and tell {friend} a one-sentence story about where it came from.
Tear paper into four small cards. Sit on the floor with {friend} and a crayon.
Draw a different dance move on each card — a spin, a hop, a wiggle, a freeze.
What kind of music plays while you dance?
Hum or sing fast bouncy tune. Flip a card and do the move.
Hum a slow floaty tune. Flip a card and do the move slooowly.
Clap a steady beat. Flip a card and match the move to your claps.
Flip the rest of the cards one by one until you have danced every move.
Bow to {friend} and stack the cards into a tidy deck for next time.
Bring four picture books and place them open in a half-circle in {room}.
Set one stuffed animal or {friend} inside each open book — those are the habitats.
Walk in a slow loop around the zoo and announce each habitat by name.
Pick the animal that looks hungriest and pretend to feed them three pretend snacks.
What special zoo event happens next?
Carry {friend} on your shoulder and march past every habitat clapping and cheering.
Tip-toe past every habitat whispering goodnight. Tuck {friend} on top of the last book.
Collect six paper cups, plastic cups, or yogurt pots and rinse-clean them if needed.
Set the cups upside-down in a {room} corner. Those are the buildings.
What kind of city is this?
Cluster the cups close together in a tight grid. Add tiny paper-square doors.
Line the cups in two long rows with a 'beach' (a folded blue cloth) between.
Stack cups in a pyramid. Tape paper antennas on top of the highest one.
Lay strips of paper or string between buildings as roads or paths.
Walk {friend} along the streets like a tour guide and name three buildings out loud.
Find a sunny spot near a window in {room} with paper and a crayon.
Stand a small object — a cup, a {prop}, a toy — on the paper so its shadow falls on the page.
Hold the object steady and trace its shadow with the crayon.
Move the object and try a different angle, then trace again.
What should the shadow drawing become?
Add eyes, fangs, and ears inside the shadow outline. Show {friend} the new monster.
Write one tiny letter inside each shadow outline. Hand the page to a grown-up to decode.
What kind of pretend snack are we making with {friend}?
Pretend to drop three colors of fruit into a blender cup. Make whirring sounds with your mouth.
Pretend to stack three layers — bread, jelly, more bread — patting each layer flat.
Give the snack a silly name. Say it three times so {friend} can remember.
Draw the snack on a paper card and write the silly name underneath.
Hand the recipe card to {friend} for the cookbook.
Gather all the stuffed animals you can carry into one pile in {room}.
Sit cross-legged with {friend} on top of the pile as the leader.
How should the animals be stacked?
Stack one animal at a time as high as you dare. Hold your breath at the wobbly part.
Set three on the bottom, two in the middle, one on top. Step back and check the shape.
Pile every animal into a snuggly heap. Wiggle in the middle yourself.
Touch each animal and say its name out loud — invent one for any stranger.
Find a balloon (or a rolled-up sock) for the indoor ball.
Mark two goals — one on each side of {room} — using shoes or pillows.
{friend} drops the ball at center floor. Crouch ready on opposite sides.
What is the rule for moving the ball?
Kick gently — the first goal scored ends the round.
Bat softly — the first goal scored ends the round.
Crawl and blow — the first goal scored ends the round.
Fold a piece of paper in half. Sit beside {friend} the design assistant.
Draw half a person along the fold — head, body, one arm, one leg.
Cut along the outline (with help if scissors are tricky), then unfold.
Color the doll's face with eyes, nose, and a smile.
What is this paper friend wearing today?
Tear a small triangle of paper, color it, and tape it to the doll's shoulders.
Draw flowers and ruffles down the doll's body. Pick a name and tell {friend}.
Stand face to face with {friend} (or a grown-up) in the middle of {room}.
Pick a starting move — a regular high-five works great.
Add a second move: maybe a low-five or a fist bump.
Add a third move: a spin, a jump, a wiggle — anything!
Pick a big finishing move for the handshake.
Run the whole handshake start to end three times. End each time with the loud clap-and-shout.
Run the whole handshake start to end three times. End each time with the slow bow.
Pile every pillow you can find into one big mountain in {room}.
Plant a paper flag (a folded sheet on a pencil) at the very top.
Walk slowly around the mountain three times to scout the climb.
Pick a 'base camp' on one side and place {friend} there.
Climb (or scoot) carefully from base camp toward the flag.
What do you do when you reach the summit?

choices.label: Slide back down on your bottom

steps.text: Stand tall, beat your chest, and let out one giant ROAR. {friend} cheers from base camp.
Sit down on the top pillow and slide-tumble back to {friend}, giggling the whole way.
Find an empty cup, jar, or bowl to be your collecting jar.
Tear paper into ten small pieces and crumple them into pretend leaves.
Drop the pretend leaves into the jar with {friend} as the field assistant.
Cut three tiny paper bugs — a beetle, a worm, a ladybug — and tuck them in.
How will you cover the bug jar?
Tear a square of paper towel and rest it gently on top. Show {friend} how the bugs can breathe.
Lay a square of plastic wrap (or tissue paper) on top. Watch the bugs through the window with {friend}.
Roll three pairs of clean socks into three soft balls in {room}.
How should the relay course look?
Line the sock-balls up at the start. Carry one at a time to {friend} as fast as you can.
Set three pillows in a zig-zag. Weave each sock-ball through to {friend}.
Set up a chair tunnel. Push each sock-ball under it on your hands and knees.
Count the sock-balls now sitting with {friend}. Cheer your finishing time.
Pick a shoebox, a drawer, or an open book as the dollhouse frame.
What kind of room is this?
Fold a tissue into a tiny bed. Add a pencil-eraser pillow.
Stand a bottle cap on its side as a stove. Tear paper triangles for pizza slices.
Roll a paper square into a tiny canvas. Set crayon-stub paint pots beside it.
Move {friend} (or a small toy) into the room as the resident.
Give a tour out loud — three things {friend} can see from inside.
Find a clear cup or jar and a piece of cotton (or torn tissue) for clouds.
Tear blue paper into tiny rain-drop scraps and a yellow paper sun.
Layer the cotton 'cloud' near the top, sun on one side, rain scraps below.
What kind of weather is brewing in this jar?
Push the sun to the front and the cloud to the side. Tell {friend} the picnic plan.
Push the cloud to the front. Tap the jar like raindrops while {friend} hides under a pillow umbrella.

templates.title: Set up a tiny lending library

steps.text: Stack five small books on a low shelf or floor patch with {helper_townsperson}.
Make a paper sign that says LIBRARY and tape it above the stack.
Tear five paper squares as borrower cards — one per book.
What special library event happens today?
Pick one book, sit {helper_townsperson} beside you, and read or tell the first page out loud.
Line the books up cover-out. Have {helper_townsperson} pick the best cover and award it a paper ribbon.
Stand in a clear corner of {room} with {friend} as the photographer.
Pretend to hold up a camera and say 'CLICK' to start a pose.
What is the photo theme today?
Hold three different superhero poses. Freeze for three seconds each.
Pose as three different animals. Freeze and make the animal sound.
Make three of the silliest faces you can. Freeze long enough for {friend} to 'click'.
Pretend to scroll through the photos with {friend} and pick the best one.
Sit at a flat surface with paper, {friend}, and a calm minute.
Fold a paper square diagonally to make a triangle.
Fold the two side corners up to meet at the top — that's a paper boat-ish shape.
Open the bottom flap and pinch into a small cup.
What goes in your origami cup?
Hunt for three pocket-size treasures and drop them in the cup.
Write or draw a tiny message on a paper scrap and fold it into the cup.
Hold the cup with two hands and present it to {friend} like a royal gift.
Find {count} small flat-ish things — books, coasters, paper plates — and a stretch of clear floor.
Stand still in {room} with {friend} watching from a safe spot.
Practice balancing the first item on your {body_part} for a count of three.
Now what? Stack more, or take a walk?

choices.label: Walk one step at a time

steps.text: Add the items one by one to your {body_part}. Hold steady until {friend} counts to {count}.
Take {count} slow steps with one item on your {body_part}. {friend} cheers each step.
Stand in {room} with {friend} as the chain leader.
Pick a starting spot and {action_verb} once in place.
What pattern does the chain follow?
{action_verb} {count} times in a straight line. Land each one a step further from where you started.
{action_verb} {count} times in a circle around {friend}. Each one in a new spot on the loop.
Bow to {friend} and shout the number out loud — {count}!
Pick a starting {prop} and place it on the floor in one corner of {room}.
Find a second prop-like object and lay it beside the first, just touching.
Keep adding objects in a long line — anything small and safe — for {count} pieces.
Walk slowly along the line beside {friend} and name each object out loud.
What is the chain for?
Carry {friend} along the chain in tiny hops, stopping once at every object.
Make up a one-sentence chapter for each object as you walk past. End with the last object as the big twist.
Pick {count} flat spots in {room} — corners, chairs, books on the floor.
At each spot, place one object that feels {adjective}.
Walk {friend} from station to station and touch each object once.
What do we do at the stations?
Spin one full turn at each station with {friend} held high.
Lean in and whisper one secret to {friend} at each station.
Strike a different pose at each station and freeze for two seconds.
Pick a paper, a tube, or a sock — your craft needs to help you {action_verb}.
How will the craft work?
Roll the paper into a hand-held tool. Tape it shut and decorate the grip.
Cut and tape a paper band that fits on your {body_part}. Add a little flair.
Use your new craft to {action_verb} three times in front of {friend}.
Bow. Tell {friend} what to call this invention.
Lay paper down in {room}. {guide_mentor} is the curator.
Press your {body_part} gently on a damp paper towel, then onto the dry paper to leave a print.
Make {count} prints in a row across the page.
How do we label the museum prints?
Write a number under each print. Walk {guide_mentor} along the gallery and read each number aloud.
Invent a silly monster name for each print and write or whisper it to {guide_mentor}.
Gather {count} prop-style objects in a pile in {room}.
Sit {quest_giver} on a chair as the royal audience.
What kind of parade is this?
Hold each prop high and march past {quest_giver} stamping your feet. Cycle through all {count}.
Hold each prop with two hands and glide past {quest_giver} on tip-toe. Cycle through all {count}.
Stack the props in front of {quest_giver} and give a royal salute.
Tear a sheet of paper into {count} smaller squares. Sit beside {friend}.
On the first square, draw something that looks {adjective}.
On the second, draw something else that looks {adjective} — but in a different way.
Keep going until all {count} squares are full of {adjective} pictures.
How do we display the pictures?

choices.label: Tape them in a long row

steps.text: Tape the squares in a long row on a wall or floor. Walk {friend} along like a gallery tour.
Fan the squares around {friend} in a half-circle. Spin slowly and call out which one is the {adjective}-est.
Sit very still in the middle of {room} with {friend} and a pencil.
Close your eyes for ten quiet seconds and listen.
Open your eyes. Tell {friend} {count} sounds you heard.
Draw a quick map of {room} with a tiny picture for each sound's source.
What should the sound map become?
Cross out the loudest sound. Whisper a goodnight to each remaining one with {friend}.
Tip-toe to each sound's source and tap it once. {friend} keeps score on the map.
Pick a small soft prop — a sock, a paper ball, a small pillow.
Set the prop on the floor in {room}. {friend} watches from the side.
How tricky should this be?
Use only your {body_part} to roll the prop across {room} to {friend}.
Use only your {body_part} to lift the prop onto a chair near {friend}. No hands cheating!
Balance the prop on your {body_part}, then move three steps without dropping it.
Pick one tidy mission for {room}.

choices.label: Three soft things to a basket

steps.text: Find {count} soft things and carry them to a basket. {helper_townsperson} cheers each one home.
Find {count} stray books and slot them onto a shelf. {helper_townsperson} reads the title aloud each time.
Sit knee-to-knee with {friend} in {room}.
How do we earn a smile?
Tell {friend} the silliest one-line joke you know. Wait for the giggle.
Wiggle your whole body for ten counts. {friend}'s smile is your prize.
Stand in the doorway of {room} with {friend} on alert.
Look around and call out the first round thing you spot.
What now?
Pick up {count} round things and pile them in front of {friend}. Take a bow.
Point at each round thing and count out loud until you reach {count}.
Empty a sock drawer (or a sock pile) onto the floor of {room}.
How do we sort the socks?
Match each sock to its twin. Roll each pair into a ball and stack them up. {helper_townsperson} keeps the count.
Line the socks up in rainbow order — red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple. Show {helper_townsperson} the streak.
Take one sheet of paper. Sit beside {friend}.
Fold the paper in half across the long way, then fold the top corners down to the center.
How does the hat finish?

choices.label: Wear it as a sailor hat
Flip it for a wizard hat

steps.text: Fold the bottom flaps up. Pop the hat open and put it on. Salute {friend}.
Stand the hat tall like a cone and balance it on your head. Wave a pretend wand at {friend}.
Clear a big rectangle of floor in {room}. {quest_giver} is the royal architect.
Carry over four cushions for the foundation walls.
Place the cushions in a hollow square. Step inside to test the size.
Add a row of books along the top of two cushions to make taller walls.
Drape a blanket across two walls as the throne-room roof.
Place a small stool or pile of pillows inside as the throne for {quest_giver}.
What kind of tower does the castle need?
Stack three more pillows or boxes by one wall. The taller the better.
Lay two cushions side by side across the entrance with a gap to crawl through.
Tuck a blanket-covered chair behind the back wall. Mark the entrance with a {prop}.
Cut paper triangles and tape them to walls as banners. Pick royal colors.
Crown {quest_giver} on the throne with a paper-strip crown and bow three times.

templates.title: Set up a make-believe market stall

steps.text: Pick a small table or low chair-back as your market counter.
Tear paper into ten small price tags and set them in a stack.
Gather six pretend goods — fruit toys, blocks, paper-ball muffins, anything.
Arrange the goods in a tidy row across the counter.
Write or draw a price on each tag and place it in front of its goods.
Tape a paper sign above the counter with the stall's name.
What is the stall's specialty today?
Set a fluffy pillow loaf in the center. Pretend to slice it for any customer.
Cut paper diamonds and color them shiny. Lay them on a folded napkin tray.
{helper_townsperson} comes to shop. Greet them, show three options, and wrap their pick in paper.
Hand {helper_townsperson} the package and wave goodbye. Count the pretend coins they 'paid'.
Find a sink, a bathtub, or a wide bowl of water. Bring two sheets of paper and {friend}.
Fold the first sheet in half the long way, then unfold.
Fold the top corners down to the center crease — that's the boat's nose.
Fold the bottom strips up on each side to make the boat's rim.
Pull the rim apart and pinch the bottom flat — your first boat is ready.
Repeat the folds with the second sheet to make boat number two.
Color the sails or hulls so the boats look different.
What kind of race is this?
Set both boats at one end. Blow gently and cheer the first boat to touch the far wall.
Stir the water gently in a circle. Count how many laps each boat makes before stopping.
Lift the winning boat out and present it to {friend}. Drip-dry it on a paper towel.
Set out a brush, a small towel, and a paper-bow ribbon in {room}. {friend} is first in line.
Greet {friend} at the salon door and lead them to the towel mat.
Brush {friend}'s fur (or fabric) gently in long strokes. Hum a salon song.
Pretend to wash {friend}'s ears with a small dab of pretend shampoo.
Pat {friend} dry with the towel. Spin them gently to check the look.
What style does {friend} want today?
Tease the fur outward with the brush. Tie the paper bow in a big poof on top.
Press the fur smooth with your hand. Tie the paper bow flat at the side.
Brush in zig-zag swoops. Tie the paper bow in a crooked, fun knot.
Hold {friend} up to a mirror (or pretend mirror) for the big reveal.
Wave {friend} out the door and call the next pretend pet for an appointment.
Tape three sheets of paper edge-to-edge along a wall or floor strip in {room}.
Sit at one end with {friend} and a stack of crayons or markers.
Draw a long horizontal line across all three sheets — that is the ground.
On the first sheet, draw two small houses with square windows.
On the second sheet, draw a park with three trees and a bench.
On the third sheet, draw a tall building or two — the downtown skyline.
Add roads connecting the buildings along the ground line.
What's the weather in your paper town?
Draw a big sun in the corner and a couple of fluffy clouds across the sky.
Draw rain lines from the clouds and tiny puddles along the road.
Cover the ground with little dots of snow and add a snowman beside the park.
Draw three stick-figure people doing different things on the road.
Walk {friend} from one end of the mural to the other. Tell the story of each piece.
